title: Puppy Teething and Bleeding Gums: A Guide for Pet Parents
meta_desc: Is your puppy bleeding from the mouth while teething? Learn why this happens, why it's usually low urgency, and how photos help your vet triage.
content_html:
What is Teething Bleeding?

Teething is a normal developmental milestone for puppies, typically occurring between 3 and 7 months of age. As adult teeth push through the gums and baby teeth fall out, it is common to see small amounts of blood. You might notice pink-tinged saliva, small spots of blood on chew toys, or slight bleeding directly from the gum line where a tooth is emerging or shedding.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for minor bleeding due to teething is Low. In most cases, this is a natural process and the bleeding should stop on its own within a few minutes. If your puppy is still playing, eating, and acting like their usual self, there is generally no cause for alarm. However, if the bleeding is profuse, continuous, or accompanied by extreme lethargy, you should contact your veterinarian.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of your puppy's mouth can be a vital tool for virtual triage. A photo allows a veterinary professional to see the exact source of the blood and the condition of the surrounding gum tissue. It helps distinguish between a normal lost baby tooth and potential issues like a fractured tooth, an embedded foreign object, or an injury to the tongue or cheeks. Being able to see the color of the gums also helps us ensure your puppy's circulation is healthy.

faqs.question: How can I soothe my puppy's teething pain?
faqs.answer: You can offer soft, chilled chew toys or a damp, frozen washcloth for them to chew on. This helps numb the gums and reduce inflammation naturally.

faqs.question: When do puppies stop teething?
faqs.answer: Most puppies have their full set of 42 adult teeth by the time they are 6 to 7 months old, at which point the bleeding and discomfort should subside.

faqs.question: What if my puppy loses a tooth while playing?
faqs.answer: Losing a baby tooth during play is very common. Check the area for excessive bleeding, but otherwise, it is a normal part of growing up.
